era_context: The Classical Greek world in the mid-4th century BCE, with Thebes at the height of its power following the victory at Leuctra (371 BCE). Sparta's hegemony had been broken, but no single Greek power had yet achieved lasting dominance. Philip II of Macedon was in the early stages of consolidating his kingdom. The Persian Achaemenid Empire remained vast but internally unstable. In China, the Warring States period was in full swing, with major states competing for supremacy.

events.title: Pelopidas killed in battle at Cynoscephalae; Theban power begins to decline
events.description: Pelopidas, the great Theban general and statesman, was killed in battle against the Thessalian tyrant Alexander of Pherae at Cynoscephalae in 366 BCE (some sources date this to 364 BCE). His death marked a turning point in Theban fortunes. Although Epaminondas remained alive and continued to lead Theban forces, Pelopidas' death symbolized the beginning of the end for Theban hegemony. The loss of this charismatic leader weakened Theban political cohesion.
events.certainty_note: Pelopidas' death is well-attested in multiple sources, but the precise date is disputed. Plutarch and Diodorus place it in 366 BCE, while some modern scholars argue for 364 BCE based on chronological reconstruction. The event itself is confirmed.
cross_references: -362-001 (Battle of Mantinea, end of Theban hegemony)

events.title: Artaxerxes II continues reign amid Persian instability
events.description: Artaxerxes II (ruled 404–358 BCE) was in the middle of his long reign, which was characterized by internal rebellions, satrapal revolts, and the loss of Egypt. In 366 BCE, the empire was stable enough militarily but faced chronic administrative challenges. The reign saw the rise of powerful eunuchs and court factions, which would later destabilize the succession. Despite these internal problems, the Achaemenid Empire remained the largest and most powerful state in the Near East.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Artaxerxes II's reign is well-known from Greek sources, but specific events in 366 BCE are not well-documented. Archaeological evidence confirms the empire's continued operation but does not provide year-specific information.

events.title: Philip II consolidates Macedonian power; early military campaigns
events.description: Philip II, who became king of Macedon around 359 BCE, was in the early years of his reign and was consolidating his power against internal rivals and external threats. By 366 BCE, he had secured his position and was beginning to expand Macedonian territory. His military innovations—the sarissa phalanx and professional standing army—were being developed during this period. Philip was also establishing diplomatic and dynastic ties with neighboring powers.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Philip's early reign is uncertain. Sources disagree on the dates of specific campaigns and consolidation efforts. The general trajectory is clear, but year-specific events are difficult to pin down.

events.title: Warring States period: Qin and Chu continue expansion
events.description: During the Warring States period, the major Chinese states continued their competition for supremacy. Around 366 BCE, Qin was beginning to emerge as a major power in the west, while Chu dominated the south. The state of Zhao in the north was also expanding. This period saw continuous military innovation, including improvements in crossbow technology and chariot tactics. Philosophical schools like Legalism and Confucianism were developing in response to the chaos of the era.
key_figures: Various rulers of Qin, Chu, Zhao, and other states

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Archaeological evidence from Warring States sites

events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology for the Warring States period is uncertain by several years. The Shiji was compiled centuries later. Archaeological evidence provides material confirmation but not precise year-by-year dating.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Pelopidas' death is disputed. While Plutarch and Diodorus place it in 366 BCE, some modern scholars (based on chronological reconstruction) argue for 364 BCE. This is a significant uncertainty. The chronology of Philip II's early reign is also debated; some sources suggest different dates for his consolidation of power. For China, the Warring States chronology is notoriously uncertain, with the Bamboo Annals and Shiji sometimes disagreeing by several years.
historiographic_note: The year 366 BCE is moderately well-documented for the Greek world, primarily through later compilations. Contemporary sources are limited. For Persia, we rely on Greek accounts and archaeological evidence. Chinese sources are entirely post-hoc compilations with uncertain chronology. The historical record is heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Near East. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no surviving written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Pelopidas' death weakened Theban leadership, contributing to the decline of Theban hegemony that culminated at Mantinea (362 BCE).
Philip II's consolidation of Macedonian power in the 360s BCE led to his dominance of Greece by 338 BCE.
The Warring States competition in China was contemporary with the rise of Legalist thought and military innovation.
